title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable
tex_block_html: If $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable.

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_20 -- "AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling `claim_3_20_proof_*.tex` file).
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex`. Note: `main.tex` does NOT \subfile this statement file -- the
% sibling `_proof_` file restates the statement above the proof, so
% including both in `main.tex` would be redundant. This file exists for
% standalone reading / grep convenience.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_20 (statement)
% title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_20}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_20}

\begin{Rem}[AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}) and suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}. Then, for every integer $n \ge 0$, every walk
    \[
        \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)
    \]
    in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ (so $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$, and the walk constraint
    \[
        \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr)
        \;\lor\;
        \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr)
    \]
    holds at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$), and every position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the following implication holds:
    \[
        \bigl(\text{$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.}\bigr)
        \;\implies\;
        \bigl(\text{$k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}}\bigr).
    \]
\end{Rem}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html: Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg) and suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic. Then, for every integer $n \ge 0$, every walk \[ \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item i.\ (so $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$, and the walk constraint \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr) \] holds at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$), and every position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the following implication holds: \[ \bigl(\text{$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.}\bigr) \;\implies\; \bigl(\text{$k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}}\bigr). \]

tex_proof.raw:
% Refactor twin of claim_3_20 -- "AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable".
%
% Refactor: `blockable_noncollider_first`. The root row `def_3_16`
% swapped the primary/derived split of the blockable / unblockable
% classification: the LN's "blockable" disjunction (end-position OR
% some outgoing walk-edge to a node outside the strongly connected
% component) is now the primary positive predicate, and "unblockable"
% is the derived negation on the non-collider sub-class.  Accordingly
% this proof is now CONSTRUCTIVE: rather than assuming the position is
% unblockable and deriving a directed cycle to contradict acyclicity,
% we directly exhibit a witness for the blockable disjunction.  The
% cycle-construction argument of the original proof (Step 2.4) is
% reused, but its conclusion is now `v_{k \pm 1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)`
% (a sub-goal feeding the existential witness) rather than `False`.
%
% Twin of `claim_3_20_proof_AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable.tex`; the
% original proof stays in place untouched until the Phase 7 cleanup of
% the refactor swaps this twin over the original.  Statement block is
% synced verbatim from the canonical statement file
% `claim_3_20_statement_AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable.tex` -- the
% claim is unchanged by the refactor, only the Lean encoding of
% "blockable" flipped, and Step 2.1 of the original LN-proof (the
% non-collider-count derivation of an outgoing walk-edge) is reused
% almost verbatim below.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_20 (refactor twin proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_20}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_20}

% --- statement (restated, synced verbatim from
% --- claim_3_20_statement_AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable.tex) ----------------
\begin{Rem}[AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}) and suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}. Then, for every integer $n \ge 0$, every walk
    \[
        \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)
    \]
    in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ (so $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$, and the walk constraint
    \[
        \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr)
        \;\lor\;
        \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr)
    \]
    holds at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$), and every position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the following implication holds:
    \[
        \bigl(\text{$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.}\bigr)
        \;\implies\;
        \bigl(\text{$k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}}\bigr).
    \]
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
% Constructive proof targeting the refactored "blockable" disjunction
% characterisation of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}'s
% "Blockable non-collider on \pi" paragraph: end-position, OR outgoing
% walk-edge at slot k - 1 to a non-Sc node, OR outgoing walk-edge at
% slot k to a non-Sc node.  Step C.1 reuses Step 2.1 of
% claim_3_20_proof_AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable.tex (deriving an
% outgoing walk-edge of v_k from the non-collider count); Step C.3
% reuses Steps 2.3-2.5 of that original proof (the cycle
% construction), now in service of v_{k \pm 1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)
% rather than False.
Fix $n \ge 0$, a walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ (def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.), and a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$. Suppose $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ (def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.). We must show $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}, namely:
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(B1)] $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.; AND
    \item[(B2)] the blockable disjunction of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider} holds at $k$ on $\pi$:
        \[
            k \in \{0, n\}
            \;\;\lor\;\;
            \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr)
            \;\;\lor\;\;
            \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr).
        \]
\end{itemize}
Clause (B1) is the hypothesis. It remains to establish clause (B2). We split on the position $k$ along $\pi$.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Case A -- left end-position ($k = 0$).}
The first disjunct of (B2) (namely $k \in \{0, n\}$) holds directly with the $k = 0$ branch. Hence (B2).

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Case B -- right end-position ($k = n$).}
The first disjunct of (B2) holds directly with the $k = n$ branch. Hence (B2).

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Case C -- interior position ($1 \le k \le n - 1$).}
Neither end-position disjunct of (B2) fires (since $k \notin \{0, n\}$), so we must establish one of the two existential disjuncts. We do so constructively: we extract from the non-collider hypothesis an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at slot $i^* \in \{k - 1, k\}$, derive that its other endpoint along $\pi$ lies outside $\Sc^G(v_k)$ by an acyclicity argument, and plug both into the matching disjunct.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step C.1 -- the non-collider hypothesis yields an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$.}
(Reused with light editing from Step~2.1 of the original proof
\texttt{claim\_3\_20\_proof\_AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable.tex}.) Since $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$, def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.\ gives $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$, where
\[
    \mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = \Bigl|\Bigl\{\, i \in \{k - 1, k\} \,\Bigm|\, 0 \le i \le n - 1 \,\land\, a_i \text{ is an edge into } v_k \text{ in } G \,\Bigr\}\Bigr|.
\]
Since $1 \le k \le n - 1$, both indices $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ are admissible (they satisfy $0 \le i \le n - 1$), so the index set defining $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ is $\{k - 1, k\}$ and $|\{k - 1, k\}| = 2$. Hence $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ means at least one of the two walk-incident edges $a_{k - 1}, a_k$ is \emph{not} an edge into $v_k$ in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii.

We expand what ``not an edge into $v_k$'' forces on each walk-incident edge using the walk constraint of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ (recalled in the statement above). For each admissible index $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$, the walk constraint at $i$ states
\[
    \bigl(a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \;\land\; a_i \in E \cup L\bigr)
        \;\lor\;
    \bigl(a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \;\land\; a_i \in E\bigr),
\]
so $a_i$ is one of three configurations:
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(C1)] $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (a directed edge from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$);
    \item[(C2)] $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ (a bidirected edge with the ordered-pair writing $(v_i, v_{i+1})$);
    \item[(C3)] $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (a directed edge from $v_{i+1}$ to $v_i$).
\end{itemize}
Applying the ``edge into $v_k$'' predicate of def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii.\ ($a_i \in E$ with second coordinate $v_k$, or $a_i \in L$ with either coordinate $v_k$) to each configuration at slot $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item For \emph{slot $i = k - 1$} ($v_i = v_{k - 1}, v_{i+1} = v_k$):
    \begin{itemize}
        \item (C1) $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$: into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
        \item (C2) $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$: into $v_k$ (the $L$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
        \item (C3) $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$: \emph{not} into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause requires $e_2 = v_k$, but $e_2 = v_{k-1}$; and the $L$-clause is excluded since $a_{k-1} \in E$). This is the only configuration at slot $k - 1$ for which $a_{k - 1}$ fails the ``edge into $v_k$'' predicate.
    \end{itemize}
    \item For \emph{slot $i = k$} ($v_i = v_k, v_{i+1} = v_{k+1}$):
    \begin{itemize}
        \item (C1) $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$: \emph{not} into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause requires $e_2 = v_k$, but $e_2 = v_{k+1}$; and the $L$-clause is excluded since $a_k \in E$). This is the only configuration at slot $k$ for which $a_k$ fails the ``edge into $v_k$'' predicate.
        \item (C2) $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$: into $v_k$ (the $L$-clause fires, $e_1 = v_k$).
        \item (C3) $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$: into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
In summary, an admissible slot $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ has $a_i$ \emph{not} an edge into $v_k$ \emph{iff} either
\[
    i = k - 1 \;\text{and}\; a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E,
    \qquad\text{or}\qquad
    i = k \;\text{and}\; a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E.
\]
By the previous paragraph, the non-collider hypothesis forces at least one such slot to occur. We capture this in a single statement: there exists a slot $i^* \in \{k - 1, k\}$ at which $a_{i^*}$ is an \emph{outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on $\pi$} in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider} (the predicate ``$a_i \in E \land e_1 = v_k$''), with the two sub-cases:
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(O1)] $i^* = k - 1$ with $a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E$ -- the other endpoint of $a_{k - 1}$ along $\pi$ is $v_{k - 1}$;
    \item[(O2)] $i^* = k$ with $a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E$ -- the other endpoint of $a_k$ along $\pi$ is $v_{k + 1}$.
\end{itemize}
(Both sub-cases may simultaneously hold -- corresponding to the ``fork'' pattern -- but the argument below requires only that at least one slot $i^*$ exists; we may pick either when both hold, since the resulting witness goes into the matching disjunct of (B2).)

Fix such a slot $i^* \in \{k - 1, k\}$ for the rest of Case~C, and write
\[
    w \;:=\; v_{k - 1} \text{ if } i^* = k - 1, \qquad w \;:=\; v_{k + 1} \text{ if } i^* = k.
\]
By the case description, $w \in J \cup V$ (it is one of the walk's vertices) and the single directed edge
\[
    (v_k, w) \;\in\; E
\]
is on the graph $G$ (it is the walk-edge $a_{i^*}$ written with $v_k$ as tail).

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step C.2 -- the other endpoint $w$ lies outside $\Sc^G(v_k)$.}
We claim
\[
    w \;\notin\; \Sc^G(v_k).
\]
Suppose, toward contradiction, that $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$. We derive a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in $G$, contradicting def \ref{def-acylic}.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step C.2.a -- $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ supplies a directed walk from $w$ to $v_k$.}
(Reused from Step~2.3 of the original proof.) By def \ref{def_3_5}, item~vii.,
\[
    \Sc^G(v_k) \;=\; \Anc^G(v_k) \,\cap\, \Desc^G(v_k),
\]
so $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ entails $w \in \Anc^G(v_k)$. By def \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv., $w \in \Anc^G(v_k)$ means there exists a directed walk $\rho$ from $w$ to $v_k$ in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii.\ -- a tuple
\[
    \rho \;=\; (w = u_0, b_0, u_1, b_1, \dots, u_{m-1}, b_{m-1}, u_m = v_k)
\]
for some $m \ge 0$, with each $b_j = (u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E$ pointing forward along $\rho$ from $u_j$ to $u_{j+1}$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step C.2.b -- concatenating the single edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ with $\rho$ yields a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself.}
(Reused from Step~2.4 of the original proof.) We assemble the directed walk
\[
    \widetilde{\rho} \;:=\; (v_k, \;(v_k, w), \;w = u_0, \;b_0, \;u_1, \;b_1, \;\dots, \;u_{m-1}, \;b_{m-1}, \;u_m = v_k).
\]
That is, prepend the node $v_k$ and the edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ to $\rho$. We verify that $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Node and edge typing.} The nodes of $\widetilde{\rho}$ are $v_k, w = u_0, u_1, \dots, u_m = v_k$, all in $J \cup V$ ($v_k$ from the walk $\pi$, the $u_j$ from $\rho$). The edges are $(v_k, w)$ followed by $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{m-1}$, all in $E$ ($(v_k, w) \in E$ from Step~C.1, the $b_j \in E$ from $\rho$).
    \item \emph{Length.} The length of $\widetilde{\rho}$, counted by the number of edges, is $1 + m$, where $m \ge 0$. Hence $1 + m \ge 1$, so $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a non-trivial walk (its length is at least~$1$, i.e.\ it has at least one edge).
    \item \emph{Endpoints.} The first node of $\widetilde{\rho}$ is $v_k$ (by construction), and the last node is $u_m = v_k$ (the terminus of $\rho$). So $\widetilde{\rho}$ starts and ends at $v_k$.
    \item \emph{Directed-walk shape.} Per def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., a directed walk has all its edges of the form ``forward'' $(u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E$ at each successive slot, with arrowheads consistently pointing forward. The first edge of $\widetilde{\rho}$ is $(v_k, w) \in E$, fitting the slot writing $(\text{first node}, \text{second node}) = (v_k, w)$ in $E$; the subsequent edges $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{m-1}$ are the directed-walk edges of $\rho$, hence fit the same forward-slot writing relative to $\rho$'s nodes; and the join between $(v_k, w)$ and $\rho$ at the node $w = u_0$ is consistent (the head of $(v_k, w)$ equals the start of $\rho$). All arrowheads point forward (from $v_k$ to $w$, then from $u_0$ through to $u_m$); no arrowhead points backward. Hence $\widetilde{\rho}$ has the directed-walk shape of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii.
\end{itemize}
Therefore $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_k$ in $G$ of length at least~$1$ -- equivalently, a \emph{non-trivial} directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in $G$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step C.2.c -- contradiction with acyclicity.}
By def \ref{def-acylic}, $G$ acyclic asserts that no non-trivial directed walk from any node to itself exists in $G$. But Step~C.2.b has constructed exactly such a walk $\widetilde{\rho}$ at the node $v_k \in J \cup V$. Contradiction. Hence the assumption $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ is false, so $w \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$, as claimed.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step C.3 -- assemble the disjunction witness.}
We now combine the directed-edge fact $(v_k, w) \in E$ from Step~C.1 with $w \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$ from Step~C.2, splitting on the two sub-cases of $i^*$ from Step~C.1:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Sub-case~(O1)} ($i^* = k - 1$, $w = v_{k - 1}$): we have $a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E$ and $v_{k - 1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$. This is exactly the second disjunct of (B2). Hence (B2).
    \item \emph{Sub-case~(O2)} ($i^* = k$, $w = v_{k + 1}$): we have $a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E$ and $v_{k + 1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$. This is exactly the third disjunct of (B2). Hence (B2).
\end{itemize}
In both sub-cases of Case~C, (B2) is established.

\medskip
Across the three cases (left end-position, right end-position, interior), the hypothesis ``$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$'' yields (B2) in each case. Combining (B1) (from the hypothesis) with (B2), $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg) and suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic. Then, for every integer $n \ge 0$, every walk \[ \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item i.\ (so $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$, and the walk constraint \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr) \] holds at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$), and every position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the following implication holds: \[ \bigl(\text{$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.}\bigr) \;\implies\; \bigl(\text{$k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}}\bigr). \]

Proof.

Fix $n \ge 0$, a walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ (def def:walks, item i.), and a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$. Suppose $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ (def def:collider_noncollider, item i.). We must show $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider, namely:

  • [(B1)] $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:collider_noncollider, item i.; AND
  • [(B2)] the blockable disjunction of def def:unblockable_noncollider holds at $k$ on $\pi$: \[ k \in \{0, n\} \;\;\lor\;\; \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr) \;\;\lor\;\; \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \;\land\; v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr). \]

Clause (B1) is the hypothesis. It remains to establish clause (B2). We split on the position $k$ along $\pi$.

Case A – left end-position ($k = 0$). The first disjunct of (B2) (namely $k \in \{0, n\}$) holds directly with the $k = 0$ branch. Hence (B2).

Case B – right end-position ($k = n$). The first disjunct of (B2) holds directly with the $k = n$ branch. Hence (B2).

Case C – interior position ($1 \le k \le n - 1$). Neither end-position disjunct of (B2) fires (since $k \notin \{0, n\}$), so we must establish one of the two existential disjuncts. We do so constructively: we extract from the non-collider hypothesis an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at slot $i^* \in \{k - 1, k\}$, derive that its other endpoint along $\pi$ lies outside $\Sc^G(v_k)$ by an acyclicity argument, and plug both into the matching disjunct.

Step C.1 – the non-collider hypothesis yields an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$. (Reused with light editing from Step 2.1 of the original proof claim_3_20_proof_AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable.tex.) Since $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$, def def:collider_noncollider, item i.\ gives $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$, where \[ \mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = \Bigl|\Bigl\{\, i \in \{k - 1, k\} \,\Bigm|\, 0 \le i \le n - 1 \,\land\, a_i \text{ is an edge into } v_k \text{ in } G \,\Bigr\}\Bigr|. \] Since $1 \le k \le n - 1$, both indices $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ are admissible (they satisfy $0 \le i \le n - 1$), so the index set defining $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ is $\{k - 1, k\}$ and $|\{k - 1, k\}| = 2$. Hence $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ means at least one of the two walk-incident edges $a_{k - 1}, a_k$ is not an edge into $v_k$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.

We expand what “not an edge into $v_k$” forces on each walk-incident edge using the walk constraint of def def:walks, item i.\ (recalled in the statement above). For each admissible index $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$, the walk constraint at $i$ states \[ \bigl(a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \;\land\; a_i \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \;\land\; a_i \in E\bigr), \] so $a_i$ is one of three configurations:

  • [(C1)] $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (a directed edge from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$);
  • [(C2)] $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ (a bidirected edge with the ordered-pair writing $(v_i, v_{i+1})$);
  • [(C3)] $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (a directed edge from $v_{i+1}$ to $v_i$).

Applying the “edge into $v_k$” predicate of def def-edge-relations, item ii.\ ($a_i \in E$ with second coordinate $v_k$, or $a_i \in L$ with either coordinate $v_k$) to each configuration at slot $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$:

  • For slot $i = k - 1$ ($v_i = v_{k - 1}, v_{i+1} = v_k$): \begin{itemize}
  • (C1) $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$: into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
  • (C2) $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$: into $v_k$ (the $L$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
  • (C3) $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$: not into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause requires $e_2 = v_k$, but $e_2 = v_{k-1}$; and the $L$-clause is excluded since $a_{k-1} \in E$). This is the only configuration at slot $k - 1$ for which $a_{k - 1}$ fails the “edge into $v_k$” predicate. \end{itemize}
  • For slot $i = k$ ($v_i = v_k, v_{i+1} = v_{k+1}$): \begin{itemize}
  • (C1) $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$: not into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause requires $e_2 = v_k$, but $e_2 = v_{k+1}$; and the $L$-clause is excluded since $a_k \in E$). This is the only configuration at slot $k$ for which $a_k$ fails the “edge into $v_k$” predicate.
  • (C2) $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$: into $v_k$ (the $L$-clause fires, $e_1 = v_k$).
  • (C3) $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$: into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$). \end{itemize}

In summary, an admissible slot $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ has $a_i$ not an edge into $v_k$ iff either \[ i = k - 1 \;\text{and}\; a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E, \qquad\text{or}\qquad i = k \;\text{and}\; a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E. \] By the previous paragraph, the non-collider hypothesis forces at least one such slot to occur. We capture this in a single statement: there exists a slot $i^* \in \{k - 1, k\}$ at which $a_{i^*}$ is an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider (the predicate “$a_i \in E \land e_1 = v_k$”), with the two sub-cases:

  • [(O1)] $i^* = k - 1$ with $a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E$ – the other endpoint of $a_{k - 1}$ along $\pi$ is $v_{k - 1}$;
  • [(O2)] $i^* = k$ with $a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E$ – the other endpoint of $a_k$ along $\pi$ is $v_{k + 1}$.

(Both sub-cases may simultaneously hold – corresponding to the “fork” pattern – but the argument below requires only that at least one slot $i^*$ exists; we may pick either when both hold, since the resulting witness goes into the matching disjunct of (B2).)

Fix such a slot $i^* \in \{k - 1, k\}$ for the rest of Case C, and write \[ w \;:=\; v_{k - 1} \text{ if } i^* = k - 1, \qquad w \;:=\; v_{k + 1} \text{ if } i^* = k. \] By the case description, $w \in J \cup V$ (it is one of the walk's vertices) and the single directed edge \[ (v_k, w) \;\in\; E \] is on the graph $G$ (it is the walk-edge $a_{i^*}$ written with $v_k$ as tail).

Step C.2 – the other endpoint $w$ lies outside $\Sc^G(v_k)$. We claim \[ w \;\notin\; \Sc^G(v_k). \] Suppose, toward contradiction, that $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$. We derive a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in $G$, contradicting def def-acylic.

Step C.2.a – $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ supplies a directed walk from $w$ to $v_k$. (Reused from Step 2.3 of the original proof.) By def def_3_5, item vii., \[ \Sc^G(v_k) \;=\; \Anc^G(v_k) \,\cap\, \Desc^G(v_k), \] so $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ entails $w \in \Anc^G(v_k)$. By def def_3_5, item iv., $w \in \Anc^G(v_k)$ means there exists a directed walk $\rho$ from $w$ to $v_k$ in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item ii.\ – a tuple \[ \rho \;=\; (w = u_0, b_0, u_1, b_1, \dots, u_{m-1}, b_{m-1}, u_m = v_k) \] for some $m \ge 0$, with each $b_j = (u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E$ pointing forward along $\rho$ from $u_j$ to $u_{j+1}$.

Step C.2.b – concatenating the single edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ with $\rho$ yields a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself. (Reused from Step 2.4 of the original proof.) We assemble the directed walk \[ \widetilde{\rho} \;:=\; (v_k, \;(v_k, w), \;w = u_0, \;b_0, \;u_1, \;b_1, \;\dots, \;u_{m-1}, \;b_{m-1}, \;u_m = v_k). \] That is, prepend the node $v_k$ and the edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ to $\rho$. We verify that $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in the sense of def def:walks, item ii.

  • Node and edge typing. The nodes of $\widetilde{\rho}$ are $v_k, w = u_0, u_1, \dots, u_m = v_k$, all in $J \cup V$ ($v_k$ from the walk $\pi$, the $u_j$ from $\rho$). The edges are $(v_k, w)$ followed by $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{m-1}$, all in $E$ ($(v_k, w) \in E$ from Step C.1, the $b_j \in E$ from $\rho$).
  • Length. The length of $\widetilde{\rho}$, counted by the number of edges, is $1 + m$, where $m \ge 0$. Hence $1 + m \ge 1$, so $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a non-trivial walk (its length is at least $1$, i.e.\ it has at least one edge).
  • Endpoints. The first node of $\widetilde{\rho}$ is $v_k$ (by construction), and the last node is $u_m = v_k$ (the terminus of $\rho$). So $\widetilde{\rho}$ starts and ends at $v_k$.
  • Directed-walk shape. Per def def:walks, item ii., a directed walk has all its edges of the form “forward” $(u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E$ at each successive slot, with arrowheads consistently pointing forward. The first edge of $\widetilde{\rho}$ is $(v_k, w) \in E$, fitting the slot writing $(\text{first node}, \text{second node}) = (v_k, w)$ in $E$; the subsequent edges $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{m-1}$ are the directed-walk edges of $\rho$, hence fit the same forward-slot writing relative to $\rho$'s nodes; and the join between $(v_k, w)$ and $\rho$ at the node $w = u_0$ is consistent (the head of $(v_k, w)$ equals the start of $\rho$). All arrowheads point forward (from $v_k$ to $w$, then from $u_0$ through to $u_m$); no arrowhead points backward. Hence $\widetilde{\rho}$ has the directed-walk shape of def def:walks, item ii.

Therefore $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_k$ in $G$ of length at least $1$ – equivalently, a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in $G$.

Step C.2.c – contradiction with acyclicity. By def def-acylic, $G$ acyclic asserts that no non-trivial directed walk from any node to itself exists in $G$. But Step C.2.b has constructed exactly such a walk $\widetilde{\rho}$ at the node $v_k \in J \cup V$. Contradiction. Hence the assumption $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ is false, so $w \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$, as claimed.

Step C.3 – assemble the disjunction witness. We now combine the directed-edge fact $(v_k, w) \in E$ from Step C.1 with $w \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$ from Step C.2, splitting on the two sub-cases of $i^*$ from Step C.1:

  • Sub-case (O1) ($i^* = k - 1$, $w = v_{k - 1}$): we have $a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E$ and $v_{k - 1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$. This is exactly the second disjunct of (B2). Hence (B2).
  • Sub-case (O2) ($i^* = k$, $w = v_{k + 1}$): we have $a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E$ and $v_{k + 1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$. This is exactly the third disjunct of (B2). Hence (B2).

In both sub-cases of Case C, (B2) is established.

Across the three cases (left end-position, right end-position, interior), the hypothesis “$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$” yields (B2) in each case. Combining (B1) (from the hypothesis) with (B2), $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `acyclic_non_colliders_blockable`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing because the per-position predicates `IsNonCollider` (from `def_3_15`) and `IsBlockableNonCollider` (from `def_3_16`) both require it — their internal `Finset.filter` over the walk's incident edges needs decidable equality on `Node` to compute the arrowhead count $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability is required by `IsNonCollider`/`IsBlockableNonCollider`'s internal `Finset` machinery.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
theorem acyclic_non_colliders_blockable
    (G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsAcyclic)
    {u v : Node} (π : Walk G u v) (k : ℕ) :
    π.IsNonCollider k → π.IsBlockableNonCollider k
lean_blocks.explanation: This block is the statement head of the LN remark: in an acyclic CDMG, every non-collider position on every walk is blockable. The binder list reads `(G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsAcyclic) {u v : Node} (π : Walk G u v) (k : ℕ)`, mirroring the LN's layered "let $G$ be a CDMG, suppose $G$ is acyclic, then for every walk $\pi$ … every position $k$". CDMG-hood is structural (typed `G : CDMG Node`), while acyclicity is the propositional `def_3_6` predicate `G.IsAcyclic`. The endpoints `{u v}` are implicit because `Walk G u v` already carries them. The conclusion `π.IsNonCollider k → π.IsBlockableNonCollider k` reuses the per-position predicates of `def_3_15` and `def_3_16` verbatim; by `def_3_16`, `IsBlockableNonCollider` unfolds to `IsNonCollider ∧ ¬ IsUnblockableNonCollider`, so the conclusion definitionally re-asserts the antecedent in its first conjunct. No `k ≤ π.length` side condition appears: `IsNonCollider`'s own in-range conjunct makes the antecedent `False` at out-of-range positions, rendering the implication vacuous and the LN's $k \in \{0, \dots, n\}$ scope implicit. The proof body, omitted from this surface block, executes the TeX proof's Step 2.1–2.5 cascade.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: claim_3_20 — statement head; the proof body lives below the wrapped surface.
theorem acyclic_non_colliders_blockable
    -- `G` typed as a CDMG (structural CDMG-hood); `hG` carries `def_3_6`'s acyclicity proposition.
    (G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsAcyclic)
    -- Endpoints `{u v}` implicit — recoverable from `Walk G u v`'s type; `π` and `k` explicit per `def_3_16`'s binder shape.
    {u v : Node} (π : Walk G u v) (k : ℕ) :
    -- LN-faithful implication: `def_3_15`'s `IsNonCollider` ⟹ `def_3_16`'s `IsBlockableNonCollider`; `IsNonCollider`'s in-range conjunct makes out-of-range `k` vacuous.
    π.IsNonCollider k → π.IsBlockableNonCollider k

addition_to_the_LN: [acyclic_does_not_imply_directed_in_text] In this remark $G$ is a CDMG, as fixed by the surrounding context; the hypothesis "$G$ is acyclic" is to be read as acyclicity in the CDMG sense, so the edge orientations needed to define (non-)colliders are already part of the ambient structure.
design_choices:
**`Node : Type*` with `[DecidableEq Node]` auto-bound via `variable`.** Lean 4's `variable` auto-binding folds the implicit `Node` and its `DecidableEq` instance into the theorem rather than re-declaring them. Decidability is inherited from `def_3_15` and `def_3_16`: both per-position predicates need it for their `Finset`-based arrowhead-count machinery, so the signature does not type-check without it. Matches the Chapter 3 convention, including `claim_3_19`.

**Three-dash `--- helper` markers, not two-dash `-- statement`.** The `variable` line is statement-typing infrastructure, not LN-meaningful content. The two-dash `start statement` / `end statement` markers wrap only the declaration head — the `theorem acyclic_non_colliders_blockable …` line itself. Three-dash helper markers for the `variable` line are the standard Chapter 3 convention; see `def_3_15`, `def_3_16`, and `claim_3_19`.

**Conclusion is `IsBlockableNonCollider`, the positive-disjunction form.** `def_3_16`'s `IsBlockableNonCollider` reads off the LN's elaboration disjunction directly — end-position OR outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ to a node outside `G.Sc vk` — with `IsUnblockableNonCollider` carried as the derived complement on the non-collider sub-class. Stating the conclusion in the positive form lets the proof be *constructive* (exhibit a disjunction witness) rather than negation-by-contradiction on `IsUnblockableNonCollider`.

**Case-split on `k`, mirroring the tex twin's Cases A / B / C.** Cases A ($k = 0$) and B ($k = \pi.\text{length}$) pick the end-position disjuncts via `Or.inl` / `Or.inr ∘ Or.inl`; both automatically satisfy the predicate's `IsNonCollider` conjunct (vacuous via the $1 \le k$ guard of `IsCollider` at $k = 0$; vacuous via the missing `p.edges[k]?` lookup at $k = \pi.\text{length}$). Case C (interior) extracts an outgoing walk-edge $(v_k, w) \in G.E$ at slot $i^* \in \{k-1, k\}$ from the $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ count combined with `WalkStep`; proves $w \notin G.\text{Sc}\, v_k$ via acyclicity; plugs into the matching $\exists$-disjunct.

**Acyclicity discharged pointwise on the witness, not via a separate `G.Sc v = {v}` lemma.** Assuming $w \in G.\text{Sc}\, v_k$ with $(v_k, w) \in G.E$, prepending that edge to a directed walk $\rho : w \rightsquigarrow v_k$ (from $w \in G.\text{Anc}\, v_k$) yields a length-$\ge 1$ directed cycle at $v_k$, contradicting `hG`. Re-deriving this contrapositive pointwise on the witness already in hand — rather than factoring out `∀ v, G.Sc v = {v}` and specialising — is cheaper, stays close to the tex twin's Step C.2, and avoids a `Set` extensionality formulation downstream rows do not depend on.

**File-level `private` walk-position helpers, no markers.** `Walk.vertices_length_eq`, `Walk.vertices_head?_eq_source`, `Walk.walkStep_at`, and `Walk.walkStep_at_vertices` are structural infrastructure used by Case C to read off the two walk-incident edges at the interior position. All `private` with no marker comments — chapter convention for single-file proof support, outside both the public namespace and the LN-content marker scope.
